chunks.text: Why do people leave the land where they were born?
War, poverty, persecution.
Or the wish for a better life.
The reasons vary, but the decision to abandon one's homeland is heavy in every age.
From the nineteenth to the twentieth century, millions of Europeans crossed the ocean to America.
Families would accompany one another on a long voyage, and a new life would begin.
Upon arrival, immigrants first suffered from the barrier of language.
Without a single acquaintance, lonely days went on.
Many could barely afford the cost of passage.
The actual life in America was far harder than they had imagined.
Low wages, poor housing, and discrimination.
Still, many immigrants gradually adjusted to the new society.
Their children learned English at school and blended into the local community.
Becoming accustomed to their new surroundings, they began to build lives of their own.
At the same time, immigrants also kept the culture of their ancestor.
Food, music, festivals.
Their diversity enriched the society that received them.
Today, cities around the world have become multicultural societies.
For different cultures to coexist
tolerance toward one another and the effort to overcome prejudice are needed.
Whether a society can turn diversity into strength depends on everyone who lives in it.
